section.title: The Sermon on the Mount: Beatitudes and the Law
section.sectionRationale: The first of Matthew's five great discourses opens on a mountain, where Jesus, like a new Moses, teaches the character and righteousness of the kingdom. He pronounces the surprising blessings of the Beatitudes, calls His people salt and light, declares that He came to fulfill the Law and the Prophets, and then, in six contrasts, drives the commandments to their heart-deep intent.
section.thesis: Jesus unveils the life of the kingdom: a blessedness that belongs to the humble and hungry, a public witness of salt and light, and a righteousness that, far from abolishing the Law, fulfills it by reaching the heart, so that His people are called to a wholeness of love that mirrors their heavenly Father.
section.frameNote: Ten beats: Jesus sits to teach; the Beatitudes; salt and light; His coming to fulfill the Law; and six contrasts on anger, lust, divorce, oaths, retaliation, and love of enemies, ending in the call to be perfect as the Father is perfect.

units.label: On the mountain
units.note: the new Moses sits to teach

verses.text: When Jesus saw the crowds, He went up on the mountain and sat down. His disciples came to Him,
and He began to teach them, saying:

units.label: The Beatitudes
units.note: blessed are the poor in spirit

verses.text: “{{makarios|Blessed}} are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven.
Blessed are those who mourn, for they will be comforted.
Blessed are the meek, for they will inherit the earth.
Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for {{dikaiosyne|righteousness}}, for they will be filled.
Blessed are the merciful, for they will be shown mercy.
Blessed are the pure in heart, for they will see God.
Blessed are the peacemakers, for they will be called sons of God.
Blessed are those who are persecuted because of righteousness, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven.
Blessed are you when people insult you, persecute you, and falsely say all kinds of evil against you because of Me.
Rejoice and be glad, because great is your reward in heaven, for in the same way they persecuted the prophets before you.
You are the salt of the earth. But if the salt loses its savor, how can it be made salty again? It is no longer good for anything, except to be thrown out and trampled by men.
You are the light of the world. A city on a hill cannot be hidden.
Neither do people light a lamp and put it under a basket. Instead, they set it on a stand, and it gives light to everyone in the house.
In the same way, let your light shine before men, that they may see your good deeds and glorify your Father in heaven.

units.label: To fulfill the Law
units.note: not to abolish but to fulfill

verses.text: Do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets. I have not come to abolish them, but to {{pleroo|fulfill}} them.
For I tell you truly, until heaven and earth pass away, not a single jot, not a stroke of a pen, will disappear from the Law until everything is accomplished.

So then, whoever breaks one of the least of these commandments and teaches others to do likewise will be called least in the kingdom of heaven; but whoever practices and teaches them will be called great in the kingdom of heaven.
For I tell you that unless your righteousness exceeds that of the scribes and Pharisees, you will never enter the kingdom of heaven.

units.label: Anger and reconciliation
units.note: you have heard, but I tell you

verses.text: You have heard that it was said to the ancients, ‘Do not murder’ and ‘Anyone who murders will be subject to judgment.’

But I tell you that anyone who is angry with his brother will be subject to judgment. Again, anyone who says to his brother, ‘Raca,’ will be subject to the Sanhedrin. But anyone who says, ‘You fool!’ will be subject to the fire of hell.
So if you are offering your gift at the altar and there remember that your brother has something against you,
leave your gift there before the altar. First go and be reconciled to your brother; then come and offer your gift.

Reconcile quickly with your adversary, while you are still on the way to court. Otherwise, he may hand you over to the judge, and the judge may hand you over to the officer, and you may be thrown into prison.
Truly I tell you, you will not get out until you have paid the last penny.
You have heard that it was said, ‘Do not commit adultery.’
But I tell you that anyone who looks at a woman to lust after her has already committed adultery with her in his heart.
If your right eye causes you to sin, gouge it out and throw it away. It is better for you to lose one part of your body than for your whole body to be thrown into hell.
And if your right hand causes you to sin, cut it off and throw it away. It is better for you to lose one part of your body than for your whole body to depart into hell.
It has also been said, ‘Whoever divorces his wife must give her a certificate of divorce.’
But I tell you that anyone who divorces his wife, except for sexual immorality, brings adultery upon her. And he who marries a divorced woman commits adultery.
Again, you have heard that it was said to the ancients, ‘Do not break your oath, but fulfill your vows to the Lord.’
But I tell you not to swear at all: either by heaven, for it is God’s throne;
or by the earth, for it is His footstool; or by Jerusalem, for it is the city of the great King.
Nor should you swear by your head, for you cannot make a single hair white or black.
Simply let your ‘Yes’ be ‘Yes,’ and your ‘No,’ ‘No.’ Anything more comes from the evil one.
You have heard that it was said, ‘Eye for eye and tooth for tooth.’
But I tell you not to resist an evil person. If someone slaps you on your right cheek, turn to him the other also;
if someone wants to sue you and take your tunic, let him have your cloak as well;
and if someone forces you to go one mile, go with him two miles.
Give to the one who asks you, and do not turn away from the one who wants to borrow from you.

units.label: Love your enemies
units.note: be perfect as your Father is perfect

verses.text: You have heard that it was said, ‘Love your neighbor’ and ‘Hate your enemy.’
But I tell you, love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you,
that you may be sons of your Father in heaven. He causes His sun to rise on the evil and the good, and sends rain on the righteous and the unrighteous.
If you love those who love you, what reward will you get? Do not even tax collectors do the same?
And if you greet only your brothers, what are you doing more than others? Do not even Gentiles do the same?
Be {{teleios|perfect}}, therefore, as your heavenly Father is perfect.

makarios.gloss: Blessed, happy, flourishing in God's favor. Not a wish but a declaration: Jesus pronounces the truly fortunate to be the poor in spirit, the mourning, the meek, a stunning reversal of the world's idea of the good life.

dikaiosyne.gloss: Righteousness, right conduct and right standing before God. In the Sermon it is both hungered for as a gift and lived out as obedience; the kingdom righteousness must exceed that of the scribes and Pharisees by reaching the heart.

pleroo.gloss: To fill up, complete, fulfill. Jesus did not come to abolish the Law and the Prophets but to fulfill them, bringing them to their intended goal in Himself and in the deeper obedience of the heart He teaches.

teleios.gloss: Complete, whole, mature, perfect. The call to be perfect as the Father is perfect is a call to undivided, all-embracing love that reflects God's own character, the summit toward which the whole chapter climbs.

basic.context: Jesus climbs a mountain, sits down like a teacher, and delivers the most famous sermon ever preached. He begins with the Beatitudes, declaring the truly blessed to be the humble, mourning, and hungry, the opposite of what the world admires. He calls His followers salt and light, insists He came to fulfill the law rather than cancel it, and then, in six pointed contrasts, shows that God's commands reach the heart, not just outward behavior.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the Beatitudes as descriptions of the character God's grace produces in kingdom people, not as a ladder to climb. Read the six statements that begin You have heard, but I tell you not as Jesus correcting Moses but as Him exposing the true, heart-deep intent of the law against shallow readings. The whole sermon shows the righteousness of the kingdom, which only Christ can give and empower.
basic.originalLanguages: Blessed means truly flourishing in God's favor, and Jesus declares it over surprising people. The key word righteousness runs through the sermon: it is both hungered for and lived out, and it must exceed that of the religious experts. Jesus comes to fulfill the law, to bring it to its goal, and He calls His people to be perfect, whole and complete in love, as their Father is.
basic.typology: Jesus is the new and greater Moses, giving the law of the kingdom from a mountain as Moses gave the law from Sinai, yet with an authority Moses never claimed: I tell you. His people are the light of the world, the role Israel was meant to play among the nations, now taken up in Christ.
basic.christ: Jesus speaks with a startling personal authority, setting His word above what was said to the ancients and making loyalty to Himself the measure of blessing. He is the one who truly fulfills the law, the only one who perfectly embodies the righteousness He describes. He alone lives the Beatitudes fully, and by His Spirit He forms them in His people.
basic.god: The sermon reveals a Father who blesses the humble, rewards the persecuted, and pours sun and rain on the evil and the good alike. His character is the standard: His people are to be merciful, pure, peacemaking, and loving toward enemies because that is what He is like. His kingdom turns the world's values upside down.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 37 lies behind the meek inheriting the earth. Isaiah 61 shapes the blessing on those who mourn. Leviticus 19 gives love your neighbor, which Jesus extends to enemies. And Romans 13 says love fulfills the law, echoing the sermon's heart.
basic.oneStory: On the mountain the King describes life under His reign. The blessings He pronounces overturn every worldly measure of success, and the righteousness He requires goes deeper than any rule-keeping, all the way to the heart. No one can live this but Christ, who fulfills the law for us and, by His Spirit, begins to shape His own character in His people until they love as their Father loves.

context.literary: This is the opening and longest section of the first discourse, chapters 5 to 7. It moves from the Beatitudes, which describe kingdom character, through the salt and light images, to the programmatic statement about fulfilling the law, and then to six illustrations of the deeper righteousness. Matthew deliberately frames Jesus as a new Moses on a mountain, and the Sermon as the charter of the kingdom.
context.historical: Rabbis taught seated, and going up on a mountain evokes Sinai. The scribes and Pharisees were the recognized authorities on the law, admired for their careful observance, which makes the demand to exceed their righteousness startling. The examples, spitting the word Raca, oaths by heaven or Jerusalem, the extra mile of Roman impressment, reflect concrete first-century realities.
context.canonical: The Sermon stands in continuity with the law and prophets, which Jesus fulfills rather than abolishes. It develops Old Testament themes: the blessedness of the righteous, the call to reflect God's holiness, and love of neighbor. The New Testament echoes it repeatedly, and it has shaped Christian ethics ever since as the portrait of the kingdom life.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To set out the character, witness, and righteousness of the kingdom under the authority of the King, showing that true obedience is a matter of the heart, that Jesus fulfills rather than cancels the law, and that His people are to reflect the very character of their heavenly Father.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The Beatitudes describe the blessedness of kingdom people; the six contrasts and the closing call are prescriptive, teaching how the redeemed are to live. These commands are not a way to earn salvation but the shape of life for those already welcomed into the kingdom by grace, and they expose our need of Christ.

debates.question: What does Jesus mean by fulfilling the Law and the Prophets?

views.label: Bringing the law to its intended goal in Himself
views.case: Jesus completes what the law and prophets pointed toward, embodying and accomplishing their aim; He does not discard the Old Testament but brings it to fruition in His person and teaching.

views.label: Establishing the law's true, heart-deep meaning
views.case: Fulfilling includes teaching and living out the full, God-intended sense of the commandments against shallow readings, so that the deeper righteousness of the heart is upheld, not lowered.

debates.bearing: Both guard against two errors, that Jesus abolishes the Old Testament or that He merely repeats it; He brings it to its goal and reveals its depth, and only in Him is it truly kept.

debates.question: How are the demands to turn the cheek, love enemies, and be perfect to be lived?

views.label: A real ethic of the kingdom, empowered by grace
views.case: Jesus genuinely calls His people to non-retaliation and enemy-love as the Spirit-shaped life of the kingdom, reflecting the Father who blesses the evil and the good; it is demanding but meant to be lived.

views.label: An ideal that also exposes sin and drives us to Christ
views.case: The height of the standard, culminating in be perfect, shows no one can attain it by self-effort, humbling us, revealing our need of grace, and sending us to the Savior who alone fulfilled it.

debates.bearing: Both are true together: the Sermon is a real way of life for the redeemed and a mirror that drives self-righteous hearts to depend wholly on Christ.

study.originalLanguages: Jesus pronounces people makarios, truly blessed and flourishing in God's favor, and startlingly He names the poor in spirit, the mourning, and the persecuted. The thread of dikaiosyne, righteousness, runs through the chapter: it is hungered for, suffered for, and must exceed that of the scribes and Pharisees by reaching the heart. Jesus insists He came not to abolish but to pleroo, to fulfill, the Law and the Prophets, bringing them to their goal. And the chapter climbs to teleios, the call to be perfect, whole and complete in love, as the heavenly Father is perfect.

typology.type: The new Moses on the mountain
typology.fulfillment: As Moses gave the law from Sinai, Jesus gives the law of the kingdom from the mountain, but with His own authority, I tell you, as the greater than Moses.
Israel's calling to be a light to the nations, Isaiah 49:6, is taken up by Jesus' disciples who let their light shine before men.
The promise of Psalm 37:11 to the humble is claimed for the citizens of Christ's kingdom, who will inherit the renewed creation.

study.christ: Christ towers over this sermon. He teaches with an authority that belongs to God alone, repeatedly setting His own word, I tell you, above what was said to the ancients, and making blessing hinge on loyalty to Himself. He is the only one who has ever perfectly lived the Beatitudes: truly poor in spirit, meek, pure in heart, the peacemaker persecuted for righteousness who prayed for His enemies from the cross. He fulfills the law we have broken and, by His Spirit, begins to reproduce His own character in His people. Every command here is first a portrait of Him.
study.god: The sermon is saturated with the Father. He is the one whose kingdom, comfort, and reward belong to the blessed; the one whose children are peacemakers; the one whose glory is the goal of our shining light. His character sets the standard for ours: we are to be merciful, pure, and loving to enemies because He causes His sun to rise on the evil and the good and sends rain on the just and the unjust. To be His children is to come to resemble Him, and the whole chapter calls us Godward, to a love as wide and undivided as His own.

crossReferences.note: The meek will inherit the land, the promise Jesus takes up in the third Beatitude.
Good news to the poor and comfort to those who mourn, echoed in the Beatitudes.
Love your neighbor as yourself, which Jesus extends even to enemies.
Love is the fulfillment of the law, the heart of the deeper righteousness Jesus teaches.

study.oneStory: From the mountain the King sets out the life of His kingdom, and it overturns every worldly value: the blessed are the humble and hungry, and the righteousness required runs deeper than any rulebook, into the heart. No mere effort can produce it. It is fulfilled first by Christ, who lived it flawlessly and died loving His enemies, and it is formed in His people by His Spirit. The Sermon points beyond itself to the Savior who both requires this righteousness and gives it, until His children come to love as their Father loves.

quiz.q: Who does Jesus say are blessed in the Beatitudes?

options.text: The poor in spirit, those who mourn, the meek, and those who hunger for righteousness.
options.note: Verses 3-10 pronounce blessing on the humble and hungry, reversing worldly values.

options.text: Those who are persecuted for righteousness, above all others.
options.note: The persecuted are blessed, but this is one of several Beatitudes, not the whole picture.

options.text: The wealthy, the powerful, and the successful.
options.note: The opposite; Jesus blesses the humble and needy, not the powerful and rich.

options.text: Only those who keep the whole law perfectly.
options.note: The Beatitudes bless the poor in spirit and hungry, not the self-sufficient law-keeper.

quiz.q: What did Jesus say about His relationship to the Law and the Prophets?

options.text: He came not to abolish them but to fulfill them.
options.note: Verse 17 states plainly that Jesus fulfills the law rather than cancels it.

options.text: He came to establish the law's deeper, heart-level meaning.
options.note: True as part of what fulfillment means, but the direct statement is that He fulfills, not abolishes.

options.text: He came to replace the old law with entirely new rules.
options.note: He fulfills and deepens the law; He does not simply replace it with new rules.

options.text: He came to set the law aside as no longer relevant.
options.note: He explicitly denies abolishing the law; not a jot will pass away.

quiz.q: How does Jesus deepen the command against murder?

options.text: He traces murder back to anger and contempt in the heart, which are also under judgment.
options.note: Verses 21-22 show the command reaches the heart's anger, not just the outward act.

options.text: He teaches that only physical killing is truly sinful.
options.note: The opposite; He extends the command inward to anger, not just the physical act.

options.text: He abolishes the command against murder altogether.
options.note: He does not abolish it; He deepens it to include anger and contempt.

options.text: He says murder is acceptable if the person deserves it.
options.note: He intensifies the command; nothing here excuses murder or contempt.

quiz.q: What does Jesus command regarding enemies?

options.text: Love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you, reflecting the Father.
options.note: Verses 44-45 command enemy-love as the mark of the Father's children.

options.text: Do not take personal revenge, but you may still despise them.
options.note: He goes further than refraining from revenge; He commands active love and prayer, not mere restraint.

options.text: Love only those who love you in return.
options.note: Jesus rejects this as no better than the tax collectors; He commands love of enemies.

options.text: Hate your enemies as the tradition allowed.
options.note: Jesus directly overturns hate your enemy, commanding love instead.

quiz.q: What is the point of the closing call to be perfect as the Father is perfect?

options.text: A call to whole, undivided love that reflects God's own character.
options.note: Verse 48 caps the section on love, calling for a completeness of love like the Father's.

options.text: A demand for flawless behavior achievable by hard effort.
options.note: It calls for God-like wholeness of love, but as grace-shaped character, not self-made flawlessness; this misses the source.

options.text: A suggestion that only a few elite disciples can attain.
options.note: It is addressed to all disciples as the Father's children, not an elite few.

options.text: A statement that God does not really expect any change in us.
options.note: The call clearly summons real, Godward transformation, not indifference to change.

questions: The Beatitudes call the poor in spirit and those who mourn blessed. How does Jesus' picture of the good life challenge the way you measure success and blessing?

Jesus said the righteousness of the kingdom reaches the heart, not just outward behavior. Where is God exposing an attitude, anger, lust, or contempt, that looks fine on the surface but needs to change within?
You are the salt of the earth and the light of the world. In what setting is God calling you to let your light shine so that others glorify your Father?
Jesus commands love and prayer even for enemies. Who is the hardest person for you to love right now, and what would it look like to pray for them this week?
The whole sermon is first a portrait of Christ, who alone lived it. How does seeing your inability to keep it drive you to depend on Him for both forgiveness and power to change?

translationNotes.note: The meek may also be rendered those who exercise strength under control, and inherit the earth as inherit the land, echoing Psalm 37:11.
Raca is an Aramaic term of contempt; the fire of hell renders Gehenna, the valley outside Jerusalem that became an image of final judgment. Some manuscripts add without cause after angry.
Except for sexual immorality preserves a recognized ground; brings adultery upon her may also be rendered causes her to commit adultery, as the BSB footnote notes.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Matthew 5:1-48. Verse text assembled by concatenating segment values in order; poem, line-break, and note markers were skipped. No em or en dashes appear. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
